committee.name: Senior Transcript Editor
interests: Whether the AI first-pass transcript is accurate enough to be worth editing rather than retyping from scratch
How the discrepancy flags (Medication, Name, Number, Filler) surface actual problems vs. flagging noise she has to skip past
How the 90-second edit lock behaves when she is pulled away mid-segment — whether she loses work or just loses the lock
Whether Find & Replace with a dry-run preview is fast enough to handle bulk speaker-name corrections across a long transcript
How segment-level undo works in practice — can she confidently fix a bad edit without digging through the whole ledger
Whether slide alignment produced by the AI pipeline is reliable or needs manual reassignment on most sessions
How readable the AI Transcript tab is compared to the STT Reference tab when she needs to check source audio
Whether she can see who made a specific correction in the session audit view without leaving the editor
How the lock takeover works when the admin needs to force-take — does she lose unsaved changes
Whether the J/K/L keyboard shortcuts and follow-video toggle actually speed up her correction workflow
How long the pipeline takes from upload to ready so she can plan her queue realistically
Whether the Discrepancies tab gives her an ordered list she can work through or just a dump she has to sort herself
decisionCriteria: The AI first-pass quality must be high enough that she is correcting, not rebuilding — net time per transcript must improve
Discrepancy flags must surface real errors at a rate that justifies reviewing them, not generating more noise than signal
Edit lock and append-only ledger must protect her work so she never loses a correction she already made
Find & Replace dry-run preview must let her verify scope before committing a bulk change across hundreds of segments
Slide reassignment must be fast — a tile picker is acceptable only if the correct slide is easy to find in the grid
Speaker relabeling must propagate consistently so fixing one segment does not leave the rest of the session inconsistent
The correction history in the audit view must be searchable by correction type so she can isolate her own edits from others'
Download output (Word, captions, plain text) must accurately reflect her edits with no delay or stale-file surprises
The editor must not feel slow on long sessions — segment rendering and lock acquisition must be near-instant
She must be able to tell at a glance whether a session is safe to start editing (locked by someone else vs. available)
Queue view must show sessions assigned to her current SOP stage, not just the three most recent globally
goals: Finish each assigned transcript faster than she could under the old workflow, without sacrificing accuracy
Never lose a correction because the lock expired or another editor force-took the session without warning
Work the Discrepancies tab as a prioritized to-do list so she can clear flags in a single pass rather than hunting
Produce Word exports that go straight to the CMS pipeline without manual reformatting
Get through her My Queue assignments in order of longest-waiting so nothing ages past SLA on her watch
Use Find & Replace to standardize speaker names and drug names in bulk without manually touching every segment
Keep the slide alignment clean so the final CMS output presents each quote under the correct slide
Understand the append-only edit history well enough to reconstruct exactly who changed what if a QA reviewer disputes a correction
Have a clear signal when a session is still processing vs. ready so she does not open an incomplete transcript by mistake
Catch filler-word residue or normalization gaps that the AI pipeline missed before the session advances past Copy Draft
objections: If the AI transcript quality varies wildly by session type or speaker accent, her throughput becomes unpredictable and the tool loses its value proposition
A 90-second lock that silently expires while she is on a call means the next keystroke either fails or gets attributed to a stale session — she needs a visible warning before time runs out
If the Discrepancies tab flags medication and name matches from a shallow dictionary rather than context, she will spend more time clearing false positives than fixing real errors
The My Queue screen showing the three most recent sessions globally instead of her assigned work means she cannot trust it for daily prioritization
If segment split/merge is off by default and she cannot turn it on, she is stuck with AI-produced segment boundaries that may be editorially wrong
The fact that slide reassignment is a tile grid works only if the grid is paginated and searchable — a long slide deck would make it unusable
An admin force-take on her lock mid-edit should save her in-progress changes automatically, not silently discard them
If the plain-text export silently removes filler words without flagging it, she might not realize the Word and caption exports differ until QA catches it
Speaker relabeling per-segment rather than globally means one careless assignment breaks consistency across a 90-minute session
The Improvements board being manual-entry only means systemic AI errors she spots repeatedly have no structured way to feed back into prompt tuning
No on-screen SLA clock in the editor means she does not know she is already overdue until she navigates away to the SOP page
openQuestions: When I open a session in the editor, how do I know at a glance whether the AI transcript quality is high enough to edit vs. restart the pipeline?
If I start editing and the lock expires while I'm typing, do my in-progress changes save before the lock drops, or do they just disappear?
Can I filter the Discrepancies tab to show only Medication flags and hide everything else while I do a focused medical-term pass?
How does Find & Replace handle a drug name that appears both in the transcript text and in a speaker label — does it replace both or just the text?
If a slide is misaligned by the AI for most segments in a section, is there a way to shift an entire block of segments to the correct slide at once rather than reassigning one by one?
The session audit shows every correction — can I filter it to only corrections I made, so I can review my own work before advancing the stage?
When the admin force-takes my lock, do I get an on-screen notification so I know to stop typing, or do I only find out when my next save fails?
Does undo/redo work across different correction types — if I did a slide reassignment and then a text edit, can I undo just the text edit without reversing the slide move?
How does the STT Reference tab differ from the AI Transcript tab visually — can I see both side by side, or do I have to toggle between them?
If the same speaker is labeled differently in two sessions (full name vs. initials), is there a way to standardize across sessions or is everything per-session?
Can I see the processing pipeline steps that ran on a session — specifically whether it went through Direct or Enhanced — so I know what kind of first-pass to expect?
If a session fails mid-pipeline and is retried, do the segments from the first attempt show up in the editor, or does it start clean?
How long does the dry-run preview in Find & Replace take on a 300-segment session before I have to commit or cancel?
When I download a Word file, does it include the slide titles as section headers, or is it just a flat stream of speaker paragraphs?
If the My Queue page excludes group assignments, how do I see sessions assigned to a group I belong to — is there a filter somewhere on the Sessions list?
Does the Corrections KPI in the audit view distinguish between corrections I applied vs. corrections the AI auto-closed via discrepancy resolution?

committee.name: CMS Publishing Manager
interests: Whether the CMS stage in the 8-stage SOP is a real handoff gate or just a label that editors skip through
How the Word export is structured — whether it arrives with consistent heading levels and speaker formatting that maps cleanly to CMS content types
Whether slide alignment in the finished transcript matches the way the CMS organizes content by topic section
How publishing-link fields on the session detail page will eventually work once the save functionality is built
Whether the export regenerates fresh from the latest corrections every time or risks delivering a stale file to the CMS queue
How the SOP CMS stage dwell time and overdue badge give him visibility into sessions aging before he can publish them
Whether the Viewer preview faithfully represents how the CMS will present the content, so he can catch layout problems before handoff
How the session code and title fields (editable inline on the Session Detail page) feed CMS metadata
Whether the caption export (.srt) stays aligned to the audio after corrections are applied in the editor
Whether bulk export of multiple finished sessions is possible, or whether every session requires a manual download from the editor
How the Word macro bundle integrates into his team's existing CMS prep workflow
decisionCriteria: The Word export must arrive with structure (headings, speaker labels, slide sections) that requires zero reformatting before CMS ingest
Caption files must stay synchronized with the audio after all transcript corrections — a drifted caption is a publish-blocker
The CMS SOP stage must be a real checkpoint that only clears when the transcript meets his team's standards, not a formality
Session codes and titles must be finalized before the CMS stage so his team is not renaming content mid-publish
Export must regenerate from the current transcript at download time — no risk of publishing a version that predates the last medical correction
Overdue badges on the CMS stage must give him enough lead time to pull in resources before a publish deadline slips
The Viewer preview must be reliable enough to use as a pre-publish sign-off rather than a decorative step
He must be able to see which sessions are sitting at the CMS stage across all active sessions without opening each one individually
Publishing-link fields must eventually persist so the CMS URL is attached to the session record for traceability
The macro bundle workflow must reduce the formatting time his CMS editors spend per session, not add a new tool to learn
goals: Publish transcripts on a predictable schedule so downstream CMS editors are not blocked waiting for late handoffs
Eliminate reformatting work between the rounds.vin export and CMS ingest by relying on consistent output structure
Know at a glance which sessions are at the CMS stage and how long they have been there before the overdue badge fires
Ensure every published transcript reflects the final approved medical corrections, not an earlier draft
Get caption files that go straight to the publishing platform without manual re-sync
Use the session audit trail to prove to editors and reviewers that the version they are publishing is the fully reviewed one
Reduce the cycle time from session reaching 'ready' to CMS publication for a measurable improvement in content throughput
Have a clear handoff protocol between the QA stage and the CMS stage so nothing lands in the CMS queue without passing QA
Attach the CMS publish URL to each session record so his team can trace any published piece back to its source transcript
Get the Word macro bundle working consistently across his team's existing tools so there is no friction at the last mile
objections: If the Word export arrives as a flat, unstyled text dump rather than a structured document with consistent heading and speaker styles, his CMS editors will spend more time reformatting than the pipeline saves
Caption exports that drift after corrections are applied are worse than no captions — a misaligned .srt file on a published lecture is a support call waiting to happen
The publishing-link fields on the Session Detail page warn that they are not persisted — that means every CMS URL his team records lives outside the system and he has no traceability
If the Viewer preview export buttons are not wired and point back to the Editor download menu, the Viewer is not actually a pre-publish QA tool, it is a display-only page with no action
A CMS SOP stage overdue badge that fires after the SLA is already exceeded is a lagging indicator — by the time he sees it, the publish slot may already be missed
There is no bulk export, so if twelve sessions reach the CMS stage on the same day, his team has to open each one individually to download — that does not scale
If session codes and titles can be edited after the CMS stage is reached, he risks publishing content with a title that no longer matches the CMS metadata he already staged
The SOP Advance button staying greyed out due to acceptance checks that never flip is a real problem at the CMS stage — his team should not need admin involvement to push a session forward
Without a built-in CMS integration endpoint, every publish is a manual download-and-upload workflow that is both slow and error-prone
If the My Queue screen does not show group-assigned sessions, a CMS-team group assignment will never surface in anyone's personal queue
openQuestions: When I download a Word export, exactly how is the document structured — does it use heading styles for slide titles and paragraph styles for speaker turns, or is it plain paragraphs throughout?

If a text correction is made in the editor after I have already downloaded the Word file for CMS staging, will the next download reflect that change, and is there any indicator on the session that a new correction landed?

How does the SRT caption file handle a segment where the editor changed both the text and the timestamp region — are the cue timecodes re-anchored to the audio, or are they recalculated from the correction?
The publishing-link chips on the session detail page warn they are not persisted — is there a roadmap item for that, or should I plan to track CMS URLs in a separate system indefinitely?
Can I filter the Sessions list to show only sessions currently at the CMS SOP stage so I can see my entire pending queue without navigating into each session?
When the CMS stage SLA fires an overdue badge, does it also appear on the dashboard SOP pipeline row so I can see it without going to the session individually?
Is there a way to download all of the sessions at the CMS stage in a single batch action, or is every export a per-session manual download from the editor?
If the session code or title is edited after my team has already staged the CMS metadata, does rounds.vin record that change anywhere so I can catch the discrepancy?
The Viewer preview export buttons warn they are not wired — is the Viewer intended to become a real pre-publish sign-off screen, or is the editor download menu permanently the right path?
Does the Word macro bundle work with our current version of Word, and is there documentation on what the macros actually do to the document before CMS ingest?
How does the acceptance-check gate on the SOP Advance button interact with the CMS stage specifically — what checks are required to clear before my team can advance past CMS?
Can I add a note on the SOP CMS stage to record the target CMS URL before the publishing-link field is wired, so that context stays attached to the session?
If a session that has already reached Complete status needs a caption correction, can it be re-opened in the editor, or is Complete a terminal state that prevents further edits?
Does the session audit view let me filter by the CMS stage transition specifically, so I can prove to a reviewer when the session cleared CMS review?
When the pipeline produces an AI Direct vs. AI Enhanced transcript, does the Word export structure differ, or is the output format identical regardless of which pipeline ran?

committee.name: Operations Manager
interests: Whether the dashboard SOP pipeline row gives a true real-time picture of where every session is across all 8 stages, not just a static snapshot
How overdue badges and the ATTN signal on the dashboard translate into actionable reassignments before SLAs slip
Whether the per-stage dwell-time data is reliable enough to use for capacity planning and workload balancing
How stage reassignment works in practice — can she reassign a stuck session to someone else's queue in under a minute
Whether the system can handle the team's peak upload volume without hitting the 3-concurrent-session and 10-queue-item rate limits during a busy week
Whether session type defaults (the 8-stage assignee matrix) actually reduce the time spent manually routing new sessions
How easily she can pull the global audit log when something goes wrong and she needs to reconstruct who did what and when
Whether the improvements board gives her a structured way to track recurring pipeline issues or is purely for individual one-off requests
How the My Queue screen helps her team members self-manage their workload vs. requiring her to push assignments manually
Whether the email notification system (when enabled) is reliable enough to replace the informal Slack pings her team currently uses to move sessions forward
decisionCriteria: The SOP pipeline strip on the dashboard must update frequently enough to be a live operations view, not a stale daily snapshot
Overdue badges must fire early enough to allow reassignment before the SLA window closes, not after it has already been exceeded
Stage reassignment must be possible from the session detail page without navigating into the SOP workflow tab so she can act on a queue problem in seconds
The session type and 8-stage assignee matrix must reduce manual routing overhead enough that adding a new session type is a configuration change, not a staff training event
The per-user rate limits must be understood clearly enough that she can set expectations with her team about how many concurrent uploads are safe during a busy period
The global audit log must be filterable and exportable so she can answer a 'what happened on this session' question without scrolling through thousands of unrelated events
The improvements board must be searchable enough to tell her whether a reported problem is already tracked before she files a duplicate
Email notifications for SOP stage transitions must be configurable per stage so she can avoid flooding inboxes while still catching the handoffs that matter most
The system must support at least her current session volume without requiring infrastructure changes that create new operational dependencies
Reporting must be honest about its limits — she needs to know which KPIs are live vs. static so she can choose where to trust the numbers
goals: Keep every active session moving through the SOP pipeline without manual intervention on each stage transition
Reduce the number of times she personally has to intervene to unblock a stalled session at a handoff
Give each team member a clear personal queue so they arrive each morning knowing exactly what they own and what is overdue
Enforce consistent session routing by locking in type defaults for the most common session categories rather than relying on tribal knowledge
Be able to reconstruct the full history of any session dispute or QA failure from the audit log without asking the editor involved
Identify which SOP stages consistently run over SLA so she can target training or staffing changes at the right bottleneck
Eliminate the informal Slack pings her team uses to chase stage owners by replacing them with the in-app reassign and overdue badge system
Manage the improvements backlog as an operational tool — filing, reviewing, and closing requests in a structured workflow rather than a shared notes document
Have enough dashboard visibility to answer a leadership question about overall transcript throughput without pulling data from multiple places
Stand up a new team member on rounds.vin quickly by relying on the type/assignee matrix defaults rather than individually teaching the routing logic
objections: The Operations KPIs on the dashboard (Unresolved Discrepancies, Avg Processing, Fusion Runs) showing static zeros makes the dashboard unreliable as a true operations view — she cannot tell if a real zero means nothing is wrong or if the metric simply is not wired

The time-range tabs (7d / 30d / 90d) and session-type filter chips on the dashboard doing nothing while looking functional is a credibility problem — her team will make decisions based on those filters and get wrong answers
The 3-concurrent-session rate limit per user is low enough that during a pre-conference week when multiple editors upload simultaneously, the queue could fill before the morning rush is over
The Ping Owner button on the SOP workflow being unwired means there is no in-app way to notify a stage owner — the informal Slack pings she is trying to eliminate have no replacement yet

The My Queue screen showing group-assigned sessions differently from individual assignments means a session assigned to the 'Copy Editors' group is invisible in any individual queue, creating a gap where work can sit unclaimed

Stage-transition emails being off by default and requiring a configuration switch means she has to coordinate with an administrator to enable a feature that should be a settings checkbox for an ops manager
No built-in throughput or productivity report means she has to export the session list to a spreadsheet and compute cycle times manually every time leadership asks about output volume
The improvements board Action Plan Builder being entirely unwired (Save Changes and Regenerate doing nothing) means she cannot use it as a real project-management tool for operational change requests
The 30-day soft-delete recovery window is useful, but only the bootstrap admin can restore — she needs to be able to escalate a mistaken deletion herself rather than filing a ticket
The dashboard SOP pipeline counts being bounded by the 50-session page limit means that once the backlog grows past 50 sessions, her pipeline view undercounts active work
openQuestions: When a session is overdue at the Medical stage, can I reassign it to a different medical reviewer directly from the dashboard SOP strip, or do I have to navigate into the individual session to do that?
The Operations KPI section shows static zeros for Unresolved Discrepancies and Avg Processing Time — is there a plan to wire those, or should I budget for building a separate reporting view?
How does the 3-concurrent-session rate limit apply when multiple editors upload at the same time — is it 3 per user or 3 across the whole team, and what happens to the session that hits the cap?
When I set up a session type with an 8-stage assignee matrix, what happens to sessions already in progress if I update the matrix — do existing sessions update or only new ones?
Is there a way to see all sessions currently past their SLA in a single filtered view, or do I have to check the overdue badge stage by stage on the dashboard?
The global audit log exports as JSONL — is there a simpler export (CSV or plain table) that I can hand to a non-technical reviewer, or does JSONL require processing before it is readable?
Can I set per-stage SLA overrides at the session level, and if so, do those overrides show up on the dashboard SOP strip so I know a session is on a different clock?
The Ping Owner button warns it is not wired — what is the intended replacement, and is there a timeline for building real in-app notifications to stage owners?
If a session fails mid-pipeline and is retried by the editor, does the retry generate a new audit event I can see in the global log, or is the retry invisible to ops?
How do I tell from the Sessions list whether a session is at a human SOP stage vs. still in the AI pipeline — the status chips say 'Processing' and 'Ready' but I need to know which SOP stage it is on
If two sessions arrive at the Medical stage on the same day and only one reviewer is available, can I set a priority order in the system, or does the queue work purely on time-in-stage?
Is there a way to filter the Sessions list by assignee — to see everything currently assigned to a specific editor across all their SOP stages?
When stage-transition emails are enabled, can I configure them per stage per session type — for example, only send an email on Medical entry for ARAV session types?
The improvements board does not detect patterns automatically — is there a way to tag multiple improvement requests as related so I can surface a systemic issue when I'm making a case for a change?
How does the system handle a session where the same person is the assignee for two consecutive SOP stages — does My Queue show both, and does the overdue badge fire independently per stage?

committee.name: Comptroller
interests: What the AI processing cost per session is and whether it varies by model, pipeline type, or session length
Whether switching from the default gemini-2.5-pro to a smaller model reduces cost enough to matter for high-volume periods
How cloud storage costs accumulate as the session backlog grows, and whether soft-deleted sessions continue to accrue storage charges during the 30-day recovery window
Whether the discrepancy classification model billing is isolated from the main transcription billing so she can track it as a separate line item
Whether there is any cost metering or session-level cost reporting built into the platform, or whether she has to reconcile billing directly with the cloud vendor
How the rate limits (3-concurrent sessions, 10-queue-item cap) affect throughput and whether they create a hidden cost by slowing production during peak periods
Whether the AI processing cost changes meaningfully between the Direct and Enhanced pipeline modes for the same session
How the word-count and segment-count KPIs on the session detail page relate to the billable units in the AI processing invoice
Whether budget overruns on a busy upload week are visible in time to adjust, or only discovered during invoice reconciliation
Whether there are any per-seat or per-user licensing costs layered on top of the AI processing costs
decisionCriteria: She must be able to forecast monthly AI processing costs from session volume and average session length, with enough accuracy to hold a budget line
Cost exposure for the discrepancy classification model must be separately trackable since it uses a different backend and billing path from the main transcription model
Storage cost for accumulated sessions (including soft-deleted ones within the 30-day window) must be estimable from the session count and average file size
Switching from AI Direct to AI Enhanced must have a documented cost difference so she can make an informed recommendation on default pipeline selection
Any per-upload AI cost must be visible at the session level, not just as a monthly lump sum on the cloud invoice, so she can identify outlier sessions that drove unexpected spend
The rate limiting behavior must not silently delay production in ways that create hidden overtime costs or missed SLA penalties without surfacing as an operational alert
No-per-seat pricing must be confirmed in writing before she approves the budget — she is not willing to absorb a per-user fee she did not model
She must be able to run a cost-per-session calculation from whatever data is available in the platform without needing a developer query against the database
Soft-delete recovery window storage costs must be negligible or she must have the ability to permanently purge sooner than 30 days when budget pressure requires it
Whether I can see cost-per-transcript and throughput trends without waiting on an operations report.
goals: Hold the AI processing budget within the approved range even as session volume grows across the annual conference cycle
Attribute AI processing spend to specific session types or upload categories so she can report cost by content line to leadership
Avoid invoice surprises by understanding which platform actions (model choice, pipeline mode, discrepancy classification) drive cost variability
Ensure the permanent purge capability is available to clear soft-deleted storage before it becomes a recurring charge
Forecast next quarter's processing cost from this quarter's session volume trend so she can request a budget adjustment before the shortfall hits
Understand the cost structure well enough to evaluate whether centralizing all uploads through one processing style reduces the average cost per session
Track down any per-session outliers (sessions that cost significantly more than average) and determine whether they can be prevented through upload settings
Confirm that the discrepancy classification backend switch to Vertex AI does not create a new uncontrolled cost center without a corresponding budget line
Keep the cloud storage line predictable by establishing a policy on how long sessions remain in the system before permanent purge
Avoid approving a tool that requires a developer to explain the invoice each month — she needs to own cost visibility herself
objections: If there is no per-session cost data inside rounds.vin and she has to reconcile AI spend directly from the cloud vendor invoice, she has no way to allocate cost by session type, editor, or content line — the budget becomes a black box

The discrepancy classification model using a separate billing backend means she is managing two AI cost centers for a single product, and without a unified view she will miss one of them during budget review

Soft-deleted sessions sitting in storage for 30 days before purge is fine operationally, but if storage pricing is per-GB-day, a large backlog of deleted sessions with hour-long video files could be a meaningful cost she did not model

If the default model is gemini-2.5-pro because large context fits long videos, she needs to know whether every session runs the largest (most expensive) model by default or whether shorter sessions automatically downsize

The 3-concurrent-session rate limit could force editors to stagger uploads in ways that extend the production week — if that creates overtime, the real cost of the rate limit is larger than it appears

No printable or exportable cost report inside the platform means every budget conversation requires a developer to pull data — she is not willing to be dependent on engineering for routine financial reporting

If the Direct pipeline (single Gemini multimodal call) and the Enhanced pipeline (transcribe + refine + fuse + align) have substantially different costs per session, choosing the wrong default at scale could be a material budget variance she should have caught at onboarding

Permanent purge being admin-only means she cannot act on a storage cost problem herself — she has to escalate to the admin every time she wants to clear a backlog of permanently deleted sessions, adding overhead to a routine cost-control action
Without a per-session word count that maps cleanly to the AI vendor's billing unit (tokens or audio-minutes), she cannot build her own cost-per-session estimate even if she wants to
If there is no sandbox or test session mode, every test upload she or her team runs to validate the system during procurement burns real AI budget before she has approved the tool
openQuestions: Is there any per-session cost data stored in rounds.vin — a cost field, a token count, or a processing-time metric — that I can use to build a cost-per-session estimate, or do I need to go to the cloud vendor invoice directly?

When the discrepancy classification model is set to Vertex AI instead of the developer API, does that create a separate billing project I need to set up, or does it route through the same cloud billing account as the transcription model?
Does the default gemini-2.5-pro model run for every session regardless of session length, or is there a mechanism to route short sessions to a smaller model automatically?
What is the typical difference in AI processing cost between the Direct pipeline (one Gemini call) and the Enhanced pipeline (Speech-to-Text plus Gemini refinement) for the same 90-minute session?
Do soft-deleted sessions continue to occupy cloud storage and accrue storage charges during the 30-day recovery window, or are the files moved to a cheaper storage tier?
Is there any in-platform report — even a CSV export — that gives me session-level metrics (processing time, segment count, model used) that I could use to build my own cost allocation spreadsheet?
If the admin permanently purges a deleted session, is the cloud storage freed immediately, or is there a lag before it is released and billing stops?

The rate limit caps 3 concurrent sessions per user and 10 in the global queue — is there any way to raise those caps if we need to process a large batch during conference prep, and what does that cost?

If I want to run a test upload during evaluation to see how the pipeline handles our content, does that consume real AI quota, and is there a way to designate a session as a test to track those costs separately?
Are there any per-seat or per-user licensing fees on top of the AI processing and storage costs, or is the pricing purely consumption-based?

When the Prompt Templates section is used to bind a custom Gemini prompt, does the prompt length add meaningfully to the token cost per session, and is there guidance on keeping prompt templates cost-efficient?
The Session Detail page shows segment and word counts — do those map to any billing unit the AI vendor uses, or are they only editorial metrics with no cost correlation?
Can I see how transcript volume and turnaround translate into cost per finished transcript month over month?
If we add editors or change the SOP, where do I see the effect on cost and capacity?
Does the reporting tie production output to what we publish, so I can judge whether the operation pays for itself?

committee.name: Administration Manager
interests: Whether account provisioning (adding a new editor, resetting a password, disabling a departing employee) can be done without calling the bootstrap admin each time
Whether the session type and 8-stage assignee matrix is something he can maintain himself when a team member leaves or changes roles, or whether it requires admin access
How the onboarding path works for a new editor — is there documentation in the help center that explains the SOP stages and the editor workflow without requiring a live training session
Whether the sign-in screen's lack of a forgot-password link and no SSO is a support burden his team will have to absorb, fielding password-reset requests manually
How group assignments work in practice — whether he can create and manage editor groups for stage routing without admin access
Whether the email notification templates for SOP stage transitions are something he can configure and test without involving a developer or the bootstrap admin
How the global audit log helps him answer questions from editors about whether a session was edited or advanced correctly
Whether the improvements board gives him a way to track and close out requests from staff about workflow friction
Whether the settings sections he can access as a non-admin are sufficient to handle his day-to-day administrative responsibilities
How the deleted session recovery window works in practice — whether he can self-serve a mistaken deletion or always needs to escalate to the bootstrap admin
decisionCriteria: Common administrative tasks (adding a user, resetting a password, updating a group) must be doable without developer or bootstrap-admin involvement in the daily workflow
The help center content must be complete enough that he can hand a new editor a link and have them understand the SOP stages without a dedicated training session
The session type and assignee matrix must be configurable by whoever manages the team roster — if it is locked behind the bootstrap admin, every role change becomes a ticket
No self-serve password reset is acceptable only if the bootstrap admin is always reachable within the team's working hours — otherwise it is a support gap
The email notification settings must be configurable at a level where he can enable the right notifications without filing a request to a developer to flip a configuration switch
Group management in Team & Roles must let him add and remove members without admin access, so stage routing stays current as the team changes
The improvements board must be simple enough that staff file requests without needing instructions, and that he can triage and close them without a specialized tool
The global audit log must be navigable by a non-technical user looking for a specific session event, without requiring JSONL parsing skills
The soft-delete recovery window must give him enough runway to catch and escalate a mistaken deletion before it becomes permanent
Whether onboarding a new editor (accounts, access, SOP training) is something I can do without IT.
goals: Handle routine account lifecycle management (new hire, role change, offboarding) without waiting on the bootstrap admin for each action
Keep the session type and stage assignee matrix accurate as team membership changes, without accumulating routing debt from outdated assignments
Give new editors a clear onboarding path in rounds.vin that reduces the number of basic questions that come to him in the first two weeks
Eliminate password-reset requests as a support bottleneck by having a clear, fast escalation path that does not require a ticket to engineering
Maintain stage-notification settings that match the team's working norms without needing a developer to change a configuration switch
Use the improvements board as a lightweight triage tool for staff workflow feedback rather than managing requests in email or shared documents
Keep the team's group structure in Team & Roles current enough that session routing via group assignment works without manual corrections
Be able to answer a 'what happened to this session' question from the global audit log without needing help from the editor who ran the session
Establish a clear policy on session retention and the 30-day soft-delete window so editors know what to do when they realize a session was deleted by mistake
Get new transcript staff productive on the SOP quickly without a heavy setup process.
objections: The bootstrap admin email gate — rather than a proper admin role column — means that if the person holding that email is unavailable, routine administrative tasks (password resets, session type changes, prompt template edits) cannot be performed by anyone else, creating a single point of failure for his team

No self-serve password reset on the sign-in screen is the most common support request he will receive, and without a built-in flow, every request goes to the bootstrap admin, adding a dependency that does not scale with team size

The session types and 8-stage assignee matrix being admin-gated means that when someone leaves the team or moves roles, outdated stage assignments can accumulate for weeks if the bootstrap admin is not promptly notified

Enabling SOP deadline email notifications requires a configuration switch that only a developer or deployment admin can flip — he should be able to configure notifications from the Settings Email section without that dependency

The role column stored in the database being ignored at runtime is a long-term liability — if he assigns someone 'admin' in the UI expecting it to grant permissions, nothing happens and the confusion generates its own support requests

The Improvements board Action Plan Builder appearing functional (with Save and Regenerate buttons that do nothing) will confuse staff who file a request and try to use the builder, generating support questions about why their work is not saving

Soft-delete recovery being admin-only means that when an editor accidentally deletes a session and comes to him, he has to involve the bootstrap admin rather than handling it himself — that escalation chain adds hours of delay during a busy production period

No way to view another user's queue means he cannot do a workload review across his team from within rounds.vin — he has to ask each person individually or go to the sessions list and mentally filter by assignee

If the Team & Roles section is not admin-gated but the Types section is, adding a new person to the people list is something he can do, but updating the stage defaults that reference that person requires admin access — a split workflow that will cause errors

The lack of any in-platform onboarding or guided setup for new editors means he is responsible for creating and maintaining external training documentation, which will inevitably fall behind the product as it changes
openQuestions: Which settings sections can I manage as a regular signed-in user vs. the ones that require the bootstrap admin — is there a clear list, or do I find out by hitting a 403?

When I add a new person in Team & Roles, they immediately appear as an option in stage assignee pickers — but does the bootstrap admin also have to do something in the session types matrix before that person can be set as a default assignee?
Is there a way to delegate password reset capability to a second person on the team without giving them the bootstrap admin email, or is every password reset permanently tied to that one account?

The email notification configuration switch that enables SOP deadline emails — is that a setting I can toggle in the Settings Email section, or is it a deployment environment variable that requires developer access?

If I remove a person from Team & Roles because they left the organization, what happens to sessions where they are still assigned as the current stage owner — do those sessions immediately show as unassigned?
Can I create a group in Team & Roles and add members to it without admin access, so that I can maintain stage routing groups as my team changes?
The global audit log requires a sign-in but no admin access — if I want to review what happened on a disputed session, can I filter the log by session ID and actor name without JSONL parsing?

When the bootstrap admin creates a login with a role label of 'admin', nothing changes at runtime — is there any plan to wire the role column to actual permissions so I can manage admin access without depending on one hardcoded email?

If a new editor joins and needs an account, what is the fastest path to getting them signed in on day one — does the bootstrap admin have to be available in real time, or can I prepare the account ahead of time?
The improvements board Delete button says it soft-deletes a request — can I restore a soft-deleted improvement request if I close one by mistake, or is that also a permanent action?
Is there a way to see which sessions are currently unassigned at any SOP stage — for example, if a stage owner is out sick and their sessions need to be covered before the overdue badge fires?
When I use the Settings Email section to edit a stage-transition template, does the change apply immediately to all future stage transitions, or is there a publish step I have to complete?
The sign-in page has no forgot-password link and no SSO — if I need to reset a password on a Friday afternoon and the bootstrap admin is not reachable, what is the fallback?
Is there documentation in the help center specifically for onboarding a new editor — covering the SOP stages, the editor workflow, and My Queue — that I can share without having to write it myself?

If the session type default matrix is updated while a session is mid-workflow, does the system notify the current stage owner that their assignment is still valid, or could they lose their queue assignment without realizing it?
